{"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-sonnet-4-6","id":"msg_012r9eCijTCR1Y8CHjMoKquA","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"tool_use","id":"toolu_01SAbFXGUiTkW3PHS1vKYYVf","name":"Read","input":{"file_path":"/app/papers/PMC3604156.md"},"caller":{"type":"direct"}}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":1,"cache_creation_input_tokens":53090,"cache_read_input_tokens":34055,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":53090,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0},"output_tokens":1,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"global"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"b13706b2-b9fb-4aae-82f6-8e30c6f92711","uuid":"686d01dd-f23c-403e-ae85-dbe00666580f"}
{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01FCCg8ZqHzxmZJfvbohfSzA","type":"tool_result","content":"     1→# Genome-wide mapping for clinically relevant predictors of lamotrigine- and phenytoin-induced hypersensitivity reactions\n     2→\n     3→## Metadata\n     4→**Authors:** Mark McCormack, Thomas J Urban, Kevin V Shianna, Nicole Walley, Massimo Pandolfo, Chantal Depondt, Elijah Chaila, Gerard D O’Conner, Dalia Kasperavičiūtė, Rodney A Radtke, Erin L Heinzen, Sanjay M Sisodiya, Norman Delanty, Gianpiero L Cavalleri\n     5→**Journal:** Pharmacogenomics\n     6→**Date:** 2012 Mar\n     7→**DOI:** [10.2217/pgs.11.165](https://doi.org/10.2217/pgs.11.165)\n     8→**PMID:** 22379998\n     9→**PMCID:** PMC3428903\n    10→**URL:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3428903/\n    11→**PDF:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3428903/pdf/ukmss-49236.pdf](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3428903/pdf/ukmss-49236.pdf)\n    12→\n    13→## Abstract\n    14→\n    15→**Aims:** \n    16→An association between carbamazepine-induced hypersensitivity and HLA-A*3101 has been reported in populations of both European and Asian descent. We aimed to investigate HLA-A*3101 and other common variants across the genome as markers for cutaneous adverse drug reactions (cADRs) attributed to lamotrigine and phenytoin.\n    17→\n    18→**Materials & methods:** \n    19→We recruited patients with lamotrigine-induced cADRs (n = 46) and patients with phenytoin-cADRs (n = 44) and the 1958 British birth cohort was used as a control (n = 1296). HLA-A*3101 was imputed from genome-wide association study data. We applied genome-wide association to study lamotrigine- and phenytoin-induced cADR, and total cADR cases combined.\n    20→\n    21→**Results:** \n    22→Neither HLA-A*3101 nor any other genetic marker significantly predicted lamotrigine- or phenytoin-induced cADRs.\n    23→\n    24→**Conclusion:** \n    25→HLA-A*3101 does not appear to be a predictor for lamotrigine- and phenytoin-induced cADRs in Europeans. Our genome-wide association study results do not support the existence of a clinically relevant common variant for the development of lamotrigine- or phenytoin-induced cADRs. As a predictive marker, HLA-A*3101 appears to be specific for carbamazepine-induced cADRs.\n    26→\n    27→Keywords: epilepsy, GWAS, HLA-A*3101, hypersensitivity, lamotrigine, phenytoin\n    28→\n    29→### Aims\n    30→\n    31→An association between carbamazepine-induced hypersensitivity and *HLA-A*3101* has been reported in populations of both European and Asian descent. We aimed to investigate *HLA-A*3101* and other common variants across the genome as markers for cutaneous adverse drug reactions (cADRs) attributed to lamotrigine and phenytoin.\n    32→\n    33→### Materials & methods\n    34→\n    35→We recruited patients with lamotrigine-induced cADRs (n = 46) and patients with phenytoin-cADRs (n = 44) and the 1958 British birth cohort was used as a control (n = 1296). *HLA-A*3101* was imputed from genome-wide association study data. We applied genome-wide association to study lamotrigine- and phenytoin-induced cADR, and total cADR cases combined.\n    36→\n    37→### Results\n    38→\n    39→Neither *HLA-A*3101* nor any other genetic marker significantly predicted lamotrigine- or phenytoin-induced cADRs.\n    40→\n    41→### Conclusion\n    42→\n    43→*HLA-A*3101* does not appear to be a predictor for lamotrigine- and phenytoin-induced cADRs in Europeans. Our genome-wide association study results do not support the existence of a clinically relevant common variant for the development of lamotrigine- or phenytoin-induced cADRs. As a predictive marker, *HLA-A*3101* appears to be specific for carbamazepine-induced cADRs.\n    44→\n    45→## Materials & methods\n    46→\n    47→### Case subjects\n    48→\n    49→We obtained approval for this study from the local research ethics committees of each site. Written, informed consent was obtained from all subjects prior to participation. Patients were recruited through centers affiliated with the EPIGEN Consortium; namely Duke University (NC, USA), University College London (London, UK), Université Libre de Bruxelles (Brussels, Belgium) and the Royal College of Surgeons in Ireland with Beaumont Hospital (Dublin, Ireland).\n    50→\n    51→All patients were of European ancestry as determined by self-report and genetic-marker analysis. Cases were separated according to the causal drug and then phenotyped into distinct hypersensitivity states of MPE, HSS and SJS based on the extent and severity of their ADR and using disease criteria, as previously reported [[14](#R14)]. In total, there were 46 ADR cases relating to LTG, of which 42 were MPE cases, three were HSS and one was SJS. Of the 44 PHT-induced ADR cases, 40 were MPE cases and four were HSS cases. There were four patients who experienced MPE with both drugs on separate occasions and were thus included in each set of analyses. Given the low numbers of HSS and SJS patients and the evidence that a common marker may exist for all phenotypes (as evidence would suggest for *HLA-A*3101* and CBZ-induced cADRs) we analyzed all cases together as drug-specific cADR groups.\n    52→\n    53→### Control subjects\n    54→\n    55→The control group consisted of a homogeneous subgroup of 1296 subjects from the 1958 British birth cohort that were selected by principal component analysis of GWAS. Controls were unscreened for AED-related ADRs.\n    56→\n    57→### Genome-wide analysis\n    58→\n    59→Genome-wide data across >600,000 SNPs were available for 34 LTG and 42 PHT cases and all 1296 population control subjects. Genotyping for all cases was performed with the Illumina® Human610–610K Quad platform at the Duke University Center for Human Genome Variation (NC, USA). We removed subjects with a genotyping failure rate below 95%, SNPs with low minor allele frequency (<2%), those failing the Hardy–Weinberg equilibrium and any that had missing genotype information in >10% of all subjects.\n    60→\n    61→### Imputation of HLA alleles\n    62→\n    63→We imputed classic *HLA* alleles for all cases and controls using MACH 1.0 [[17](#R17)]. This method has been validated as an accurate method for imputing HLA subtypes in our cohort as described previously [[14](#R14)]. The HLA-typed 1958 British birth cohort constituted the reference population for imputation. For those samples for which GWAS data were not available we used PCR with sequence-specific primers to determine the presence of *HLA-A*3101*. The PCR conditions were as follows: the forward primer sequence was 5′-GATAGAGCAGGAGAGGCCT-3′, reverse primer sequence was 5′-AGCGCAGGTCCTCGTTCAA-3′ and the cycling parameters were 95°C for 3 min, five cycles of 95°C for 15 s, 70°C for 15 s and 72°C for 30 s, two cycles of 95°C for 15 s, 65°C for 15 s and 72°C for 30 s, four cycles of 95°C for 15 s, 55°C for 1 min and 72°C for 2 min, finally 72°C for 7 min.\n    64→\n    65→### Statistical analysis\n    66→\n    67→Statistical analyses were performed using PLINK (version 1.05) [[18](#R18)] and EIGENSTRAT [[19](#R19)] software packages. For the GWAS, we applied logistic regression (using an additive model) to each of the cADR case groups in turn versus the 1958 British birth cohort controls. We adjusted for population stratification by including significant principal components (generated using EIGENSTRAT) as covariates in the logistic-regression model and used a p-value of less than 5.0 × 10^−8^ as a threshold for genome-wide significance. [Supplementary Figure S1](#SD1) (see online, [www.futuremedicine.com/doi/suppl/10.2217/PGS.11.165](http://www.futuremedicine.com/doi/suppl/10.2217/PGS.11.165)) for a plot of the first and second principal components between cases and controls. Post-GWAS annotation analysis was performed with WGAViewer [[20](#R20)]. We calculated study power using the program Power for Genetic Association Analyses [[21](#R21)]. We estimated the relative risk (RR) required for 80% power to detect an allelic association (under an additive disease model) with the following parameters: a prevalence of 10% of the ADR in the population, a minor allele frequency of 5%, an experiment-wide α level of 0.05 and the assumption that a genetic marker is causal or in complete linkage with the causal variant.\n    68→\n    69→## Results\n    70→\n    71→### HLA-A*3101 as a predictor of LTG- and PHT-induced cADRs\n    72→\n    73→[Table 1](#T1) illustrates the results of *HLA-A*3101* genotyping in LTG- and PHT-induced cADR cases and population controls. The allele does not appear to be enriched in any of the cADR patient groups. For each of the analyses considered, our power calculations indicate that we had reasonable power to detect effect sizes of clinical relevance (RR >2).\n    74→\n    75→### Table 1. Distribution of HLA-A*3101 among lamotrigine and phenytoin case subjects compared with population control subjects.\n    76→\n    77→| HLA-A*3101 | Lamotrigine–cADR cases | Phenytoin–cADR cases | Controls |\n    78→| --- | --- | --- | --- |\n    79→| Carriers (n; %) | 2 (4.35%) | 2 (4.55%) | 72 (5.56%) |\n    80→| Noncarriers (n; %) | 44 (95.65%) | 42 (95.45%) | 1224 (94.44%) |\n    81→| Total subjects (n) | 46 | 44 | 1296 |\n    82→| p-value | 0.698 | 0.746 |   |\n    83→| Odds ratio (95% CI) | 0.756 (0.183–3.129) | 0.791 (0.191–3.277) |   |\n    84→| Relative risk for 80%power | 2.204 | 2.229 |   |\n    85→\n    86→Table 1 Caption: HLA-A*3101 was found at a low average frequency of 4.45% across all cADR cases and at 5.56% in our population controls, which is comparable with reported European population datasets [23]. cADR: Cutaneous adverse drug reaction.\n    87→\n    88→### GWAS results\n    89→\n    90→We next conducted a GWAS for each of the drug-specific cADR groups as well as a total combined case cohort. The results of the logistic regression analyses are depicted as Manhattan and quantile–quantile plots in [Figures 1](#F1) & [2](#F2). The ten most significant markers from the logistic regression analyses are listed in [Supplementary Tables S1-S3](#SD2). We did not detect any variants satisfying our threshold for genome-wide significance (p-value of 5.0 × 10^−8^), nor did we observe any significant deviations from the expected distribution in quantile–quantile plots (results shown in [Figures 1](#F1) & [2](#F2)). From power calculations ([Figure 3](#F3)) we estimated that we had 80% power to detect a variant with a minor allele frequency >10% and a RR >2.5 when considering all cases together. We feel this RR is within the realm of clinical relevance.\n    91→\n    92→### Figure 1. Manhattan plots of association results.\n    93→\n    94→![Figure 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/1140/3428903/4ed59c7de3b5/ukmss-49236-f0001.jpg)\n    95→\n    96→[View larger image](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=3428903_ukmss-49236-f0001.jpg)\n    97→\n    98→Manhattan plots of genome-wide association studies analysis of (A) lamotrigine, (B) phenytoin and (C) combined drug-induced cutaneous adverse drug reaction vs controls. Genome-wide significance was taken as 5.0 × 10−8. Figure generated in WGAViewer.\n    99→\n   100→### Figure 2. Quantile–quantile plot of the association results.\n   101→\n   102→![Figure 2](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/1140/3428903/9ecc62eca396/ukmss-49236-f0002.jpg)\n   103→\n   104→[View larger image](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=3428903_ukmss-49236-f0002.jpg)\n   105→\n   106→Quantile–quantile plots of p-values from genome-wide association studies of (A) lamotrigine, (B) phenytoin and (C) total drug-induced cutaneous adverse drug reactions vs controls calculated using logistic regression and including significant EIGENSTRAT axes as covariates. The expected chi distribution is represented along the x-axis, while the observed chi distribution is represented along the y-axis. Figure generated in WGAViewer.\n   107→\n   108→### Figure 3. Power calculation for genome-wide association results.\n   109→\n   110→![Figure 3](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/1140/3428903/0bd06658fae5/ukmss-49236-f0003.jpg)\n   111→\n   112→[View larger image](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=3428903_ukmss-49236-f0003.jpg)\n   113→\n   114→The three analyses are represented with lamotrigine, phenytoin and combined drug cutaneous ADR groups with 1296 controls. Taking all cases together, we estimated we had 80% power to detect a variant with a minor allele frequency >10% and a relative risk >2.5. ADR: Adverse drug reaction.\n   115→\n   116→## Discussion\n   117→\n   118→The results presented here suggest that *HLA-A*3101* is not a clinically relevant predictor of cADRs caused by LTG and PHT in populations of European descent.\n   119→\n   120→It has been shown in previous studies by ourselves and others that *HLA-A*3101* is a strong predictor for the clinical spectrum of CBZ-induced hypersensitivity and we reported an odds ratio of 8.33 for CBZ-induced MPE for a population of European descent, while the corresponding value for a population of Japanese descent was 10.8 [[15](#R15)].\n   121→\n   122→We calculated that the current study had 80% power to detect a RR of 3.27 for cADRs based on a frequency of 5% for *HLA-A*3101*, which gives this study ample power to detect an effect size similar to that reported previously for CBZ-induced MPE. If *HLA-A*3101* is a predictor for either LTG- or PHT-induced cADRs, we were well powered to detect it. Of the 44 cases of PHT-induced cADRs, only two were positive for *HLA-A*3101*. Similarly, only two of the 46 cases of LTG-induced cADRs were positive for the allele. Incidentally, one of the subjects positive for *HLA-A*3101* in the LTG case group had a historical report of a rash with CBZ.\n   123→\n   124→Although we included HSS and SJS, the overwhelming majority of cases were of the MPE type. Given the small number of HSS and SJS cases, we pooled all cases into a single group of hypersensitivity ADR cases per drug for a better powered genome-wide analysis. We chose to analyze all cases together as cADR groups as we did not have enough subjects to run a powered GWAS for HSS or SJS alone. The GWAS results remain negative when the HSS and SJS cases are removed. Further studies of PHT- and LTG-induced HSS and SJS in larger patient cohorts is warranted, especially given that the genetic predictors (e.g., *HLA-B*1502*) can be selective for a given hypersensitivity reaction [[5](#R5),[13](#R13),[22](#R22)], and the effect size correlates with the severity of the phenotype across the hypersensitivity spectrum (as appears to be the case for *HLA-A*3101* and CBZ [[14](#R14),[16](#R16)]).\n   125→\n   126→Our GWAS results are not suggestive of a common, clinically relevant predictor of LTG- or PHT-induced cADRs in a European population. None of the variants tested satisfied the GWAS significance threshold, nor was there any clear biological candidate from within the pool of the most significant SNPs ([Supplementary Tables S1-S3](#SD2)). Our study was powered to detect a variant of minor allele frequency >10% carrying a clinically relevant genetic effect (RR > 2.5). We recognize that sample size is a major limitation of our study. This has limited our analysis to effect sizes in the realm of clinical relevance (for cADRs) and has prevented us from studying SJS/TEN specifically. We also cannot rule out genetic variants conveying smaller effects (RR <2.5) or rare variants of small or large effect, which were not captured by our GWAS chip.\n   127→\n   128→With the increased availability of whole-exome and whole-genome sequencing, it should become possible and more affordable to identify rare variants not captured through GWAS. Discovering these genetic markers would further the understanding of the functional role of *HLA* in drug response, as well as helping clinicians screen patients susceptible to these ADRs. This study mainly focused on patients with the less severe MPE hypersensitivity reaction; however, there is a clear motivation to investigate whether *HLA-A*3101* extends itself as a genetic marker for HSS and SJS/TEN caused by LTG and PHT.\n   129→\n   130→## Conclusion\n   131→\n   132→*HLA-A*3101* does not appear to be a genetic marker for LTG- and PHT-induced MPE reactions in Europeans. The predictive application of *HLA-A*3101* as a clinically relevant genetic marker for cADRs in Europeans appears to be specific to CBZ.\n   133→\n   134→## Supplementary Material\n   135→\n   136→**Background**\n   137→\n   138→**Results**\n   139→\n   140→**Conclusion**\n   141→\n   142→**Background**\n   143→\n   144→**Results**\n   145→\n   146→**Conclusion**\n   147→\n   148→## Acknowledgments\n   149→\n   150→**Financial & competing interests disclosure**\n   151→\n   152→This work was supported by grants from the Medical Research Council (G0400126), the Wellcome Trust (084730), UCLH CRDC (F136), the National Institute for Health Research (08–08-SCC), Brainwave – the Irish Epilepsy Association/the Medical Research Charities Group of Ireland/Health Research Board award (2009/001), the National Society for Epilepsy, the Fonds National de la Recherche Scientifique and the Fonds Erasme pour la Recherche Médicale, Université Libre de Bruxelles (Belgium) and supported, in part, by Award Number RC2NS070344 from the National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke. [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.humimm.2008.05.001) | [PMC free article](/articles/PMC2632948/) | [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18638659/) | [Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar_lookup?journal=Hum.%20Immunol&title=Balancing%20selection%20and%20heterogeneity%20across%20the%20classical%20human%20leukocyte%20antigen%20loci:%20a%20meta-analytic%20review%20of%20497%20population%20studies&author=OD%20Solberg&author=SJ%20Mack&author=AK%20Lancaster&volume=69&issue=7&publication_year=2008&pages=443-464&pmid=18638659&doi=10.1016/j.humimm.2008.05.001&)\n   213→\n\n<system-reminder>\nWhenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. We aimed to investigate HLA-A*3101 and other common variants across the genome as markers for cutaneous adverse drug reactions (cADRs) attributed to lamotrigine and phenytoin.\n\n**Materials & methods:** \nWe recruited patients with lamotrigine-induced cADRs (n = 46) and patients with phenytoin-cADRs (n = 44) and the 1958 British birth cohort was used as a control (n = 1296). HLA-A*3101 was imputed from genome-wide association study data. We applied genome-wide association to study lamotrigine- and phenytoin-induced cADR, and total cADR cases combined.\n\n**Results:** \nNeither HLA-A*3101 nor any other genetic marker significantly predicted lamotrigine- or phenytoin-induced cADRs.\n\n**Conclusion:** \nHLA-A*3101 does not appear to be a predictor for lamotrigine- and phenytoin-induced cADRs in Europeans. Our genome-wide association study results do not support the existence of a clinically relevant common variant for the development of lamotrigine- or phenytoin-induced cADRs. As a predictive marker, HLA-A*3101 appears to be specific for carbamazepine-induced cADRs.\n\nKeywords: epilepsy, GWAS, HLA-A*3101, hypersensitivity, lamotrigine, phenytoin\n\n### Aims\n\nAn association between carbamazepine-induced hypersensitivity and *HLA-A*3101* has been reported in populations of both European and Asian descent. We aimed to investigate *HLA-A*3101* and other common variants across the genome as markers for cutaneous adverse drug reactions (cADRs) attributed to lamotrigine and phenytoin.\n\n### Materials & methods\n\nWe recruited patients with lamotrigine-induced cADRs (n = 46) and patients with phenytoin-cADRs (n = 44) and the 1958 British birth cohort was used as a control (n = 1296). *HLA-A*3101* was imputed from genome-wide association study data. We applied genome-wide association to study lamotrigine- and phenytoin-induced cADR, and total cADR cases combined.\n\n### Results\n\nNeither *HLA-A*3101* nor any other genetic marker significantly predicted lamotrigine- or phenytoin-induced cADRs.\n\n### Conclusion\n\n*HLA-A*3101* does not appear to be a predictor for lamotrigine- and phenytoin-induced cADRs in Europeans. Our genome-wide association study results do not support the existence of a clinically relevant common variant for the development of lamotrigine- or phenytoin-induced cADRs. As a predictive marker, *HLA-A*3101* appears to be specific for carbamazepine-induced cADRs.\n\n## Materials & methods\n\n### Case subjects\n\nWe obtained approval for this study from the local research ethics committees of each site. Written, informed consent was obtained from all subjects prior to participation. Patients were recruited through centers affiliated with the EPIGEN Consortium; namely Duke University (NC, USA), University College London (London, UK), Université Libre de Bruxelles (Brussels, Belgium) and the Royal College of Surgeons in Ireland with Beaumont Hospital (Dublin, Ireland).\n\nAll patients were of European ancestry as determined by self-report and genetic-marker analysis. Cases were separated according to the causal drug and then phenotyped into distinct hypersensitivity states of MPE, HSS and SJS based on the extent and severity of their ADR and using disease criteria, as previously reported [[14](#R14)]. In total, there were 46 ADR cases relating to LTG, of which 42 were MPE cases, three were HSS and one was SJS. Of the 44 PHT-induced ADR cases, 40 were MPE cases and four were HSS cases. There were four patients who experienced MPE with both drugs on separate occasions and were thus included in each set of analyses. Given the low numbers of HSS and SJS patients and the evidence that a common marker may exist for all phenotypes (as evidence would suggest for *HLA-A*3101* and CBZ-induced cADRs) we analyzed all cases together as drug-specific cADR groups.\n\n### Control subjects\n\nThe control group consisted of a homogeneous subgroup of 1296 subjects from the 1958 British birth cohort that were selected by principal component analysis of GWAS. Controls were unscreened for AED-related ADRs.\n\n### Genome-wide analysis\n\nGenome-wide data across >600,000 SNPs were available for 34 LTG and 42 PHT cases and all 1296 population control subjects. Genotyping for all cases was performed with the Illumina® Human610–610K Quad platform at the Duke University Center for Human Genome Variation (NC, USA). We removed subjects with a genotyping failure rate below 95%, SNPs with low minor allele frequency (<2%), those failing the Hardy–Weinberg equilibrium and any that had missing genotype information in >10% of all subjects.\n\n### Imputation of HLA alleles\n\nWe imputed classic *HLA* alleles for all cases and controls using MACH 1.0 [[17](#R17)]. This method has been validated as an accurate method for imputing HLA subtypes in our cohort as described previously [[14](#R14)]. The HLA-typed 1958 British birth cohort constituted the reference population for imputation. For those samples for which GWAS data were not available we used PCR with sequence-specific primers to determine the presence of *HLA-A*3101*. The PCR conditions were as follows: the forward primer sequence was 5′-GATAGAGCAGGAGAGGCCT-3′, reverse primer sequence was 5′-AGCGCAGGTCCTCGTTCAA-3′ and the cycling parameters were 95°C for 3 min, five cycles of 95°C for 15 s, 70°C for 15 s and 72°C for 30 s, two cycles of 95°C for 15 s, 65°C for 15 s and 72°C for 30 s, four cycles of 95°C for 15 s, 55°C for 1 min and 72°C for 2 min, finally 72°C for 7 min.\n\n### Statistical analysis\n\nStatistical analyses were performed using PLINK (version 1.05) [[18](#R18)] and EIGENSTRAT [[19](#R19)] software packages. For the GWAS, we applied logistic regression (using an additive model) to each of the cADR case groups in turn versus the 1958 British birth cohort controls. We adjusted for population stratification by including significant principal components (generated using EIGENSTRAT) as covariates in the logistic-regression model and used a p-value of less than 5.0 × 10^−8^ as a threshold for genome-wide significance. [Supplementary Figure S1](#SD1) (see online, [www.futuremedicine.com/doi/suppl/10.2217/PGS.11.165](http://www.futuremedicine.com/doi/suppl/10.2217/PGS.11.165)) for a plot of the first and second principal components between cases and controls. Post-GWAS annotation analysis was performed with WGAViewer [[20](#R20)]. We calculated study power using the program Power for Genetic Association Analyses [[21](#R21)]. We estimated the relative risk (RR) required for 80% power to detect an allelic association (under an additive disease model) with the following parameters: a prevalence of 10% of the ADR in the population, a minor allele frequency of 5%, an experiment-wide α level of 0.05 and the assumption that a genetic marker is causal or in complete linkage with the causal variant.\n\n## Results\n\n### HLA-A*3101 as a predictor of LTG- and PHT-induced cADRs\n\n[Table 1](#T1) illustrates the results of *HLA-A*3101* genotyping in LTG- and PHT-induced cADR cases and population controls. The allele does not appear to be enriched in any of the cADR patient groups. For each of the analyses considered, our power calculations indicate that we had reasonable power to detect effect sizes of clinical relevance (RR >2).\n\n### Table 1. Distribution of HLA-A*3101 among lamotrigine and phenytoin case subjects compared with population control subjects.\n\n| HLA-A*3101 | Lamotrigine–cADR cases | Phenytoin–cADR cases | Controls |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| Carriers (n; %) | 2 (4.35%) | 2 (4.55%) | 72 (5.56%) |\n| Noncarriers (n; %) | 44 (95.65%) | 42 (95.45%) | 1224 (94.44%) |\n| Total subjects (n) | 46 | 44 | 1296 |\n| p-value | 0.698 | 0.746 |   |\n| Odds ratio (95% CI) | 0.756 (0.183–3.129) | 0.791 (0.191–3.277) |   |\n| Relative risk for 80%power | 2.204 | 2.229 |   |\n\nTable 1 Caption: HLA-A*3101 was found at a low average frequency of 4.45% across all cADR cases and at 5.56% in our population controls, which is comparable with reported European population datasets [23]. cADR: Cutaneous adverse drug reaction.\n\n### GWAS results\n\nWe next conducted a GWAS for each of the drug-specific cADR groups as well as a total combined case cohort. The results of the logistic regression analyses are depicted as Manhattan and quantile–quantile plots in [Figures 1](#F1) & [2](#F2). The ten most significant markers from the logistic regression analyses are listed in [Supplementary Tables S1-S3](#SD2). We did not detect any variants satisfying our threshold for genome-wide significance (p-value of 5.0 × 10^−8^), nor did we observe any significant deviations from the expected distribution in quantile–quantile plots (results shown in [Figures 1](#F1) & [2](#F2)). From power calculations ([Figure 3](#F3)) we estimated that we had 80% power to detect a variant with a minor allele frequency >10% and a RR >2.5 when considering all cases together. We feel this RR is within the realm of clinical relevance.\n\n### Figure 1. Manhattan plots of association results.\n\n![Figure 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/1140/3428903/4ed59c7de3b5/ukmss-49236-f0001.jpg)\n\n[View larger image](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=3428903_ukmss-49236-f0001.jpg)\n\nManhattan plots of genome-wide association studies analysis of (A) lamotrigine, (B) phenytoin and (C) combined drug-induced cutaneous adverse drug reaction vs controls. Genome-wide significance was taken as 5.0 × 10−8. Figure generated in WGAViewer.\n\n### Figure 2. Quantile–quantile plot of the association results.\n\n![Figure 2](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/1140/3428903/9ecc62eca396/ukmss-49236-f0002.jpg)\n\n[View larger image](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=3428903_ukmss-49236-f0002.jpg)\n\nQuantile–quantile plots of p-values from genome-wide association studies of (A) lamotrigine, (B) phenytoin and (C) total drug-induced cutaneous adverse drug reactions vs controls calculated using logistic regression and including significant EIGENSTRAT axes as covariates. The expected chi distribution is represented along the x-axis, while the observed chi distribution is represented along the y-axis. Figure generated in WGAViewer.\n\n### Figure 3. Power calculation for genome-wide association results.\n\n![Figure 3](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/1140/3428903/0bd06658fae5/ukmss-49236-f0003.jpg)\n\n[View larger image](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=3428903_ukmss-49236-f0003.jpg)\n\nThe three analyses are represented with lamotrigine, phenytoin and combined drug cutaneous ADR groups with 1296 controls. Taking all cases together, we estimated we had 80% power to detect a variant with a minor allele frequency >10% and a relative risk >2.5. ADR: Adverse drug reaction.\n\n## Discussion\n\nThe results presented here suggest that *HLA-A*3101* is not a clinically relevant predictor of cADRs caused by LTG and PHT in populations of European descent.\n\nIt has been shown in previous studies by ourselves and others that *HLA-A*3101* is a strong predictor for the clinical spectrum of CBZ-induced hypersensitivity and we reported an odds ratio of 8.33 for CBZ-induced MPE for a population of European descent, while the corresponding value for a population of Japanese descent was 10.8 [[15](#R15)].\n\nWe calculated that the current study had 80% power to detect a RR of 3.27 for cADRs based on a frequency of 5% for *HLA-A*3101*, which gives this study ample power to detect an effect size similar to that reported previously for CBZ-induced MPE. If *HLA-A*3101* is a predictor for either LTG- or PHT-induced cADRs, we were well powered to detect it. Of the 44 cases of PHT-induced cADRs, only two were positive for *HLA-A*3101*. Similarly, only two of the 46 cases of LTG-induced cADRs were positive for the allele. Incidentally, one of the subjects positive for *HLA-A*3101* in the LTG case group had a historical report of a rash with CBZ.\n\nAlthough we included HSS and SJS, the overwhelming majority of cases were of the MPE type. Given the small number of HSS and SJS cases, we pooled all cases into a single group of hypersensitivity ADR cases per drug for a better powered genome-wide analysis. We chose to analyze all cases together as cADR groups as we did not have enough subjects to run a powered GWAS for HSS or SJS alone. The GWAS results remain negative when the HSS and SJS cases are removed. Further studies of PHT- and LTG-induced HSS and SJS in larger patient cohorts is warranted, especially given that the genetic predictors (e.g., *HLA-B*1502*) can be selective for a given hypersensitivity reaction [[5](#R5),[13](#R13),[22](#R22)], and the effect size correlates with the severity of the phenotype across the hypersensitivity spectrum (as appears to be the case for *HLA-A*3101* and CBZ [[14](#R14),[16](#R16)]).\n\nOur GWAS results are not suggestive of a common, clinically relevant predictor of LTG- or PHT-induced cADRs in a European population. None of the variants tested satisfied the GWAS significance threshold, nor was there any clear biological candidate from within the pool of the most significant SNPs ([Supplementary Tables S1-S3](#SD2)). Our study was powered to detect a variant of minor allele frequency >10% carrying a clinically relevant genetic effect (RR > 2.5). We recognize that sample size is a major limitation of our study. This has limited our analysis to effect sizes in the realm of clinical relevance (for cADRs) and has prevented us from studying SJS/TEN specifically. We also cannot rule out genetic variants conveying smaller effects (RR <2.5) or rare variants of small or large effect, which were not captured by our GWAS chip.\n\nWith the increased availability of whole-exome and whole-genome sequencing, it should become possible and more affordable to identify rare variants not captured through GWAS. Discovering these genetic markers would further the understanding of the functional role of *HLA* in drug response, as well as helping clinicians screen patients susceptible to these ADRs. This study mainly focused on patients with the less severe MPE hypersensitivity reaction; however, there is a clear motivation to investigate whether *HLA-A*3101* extends itself as a genetic marker for HSS and SJS/TEN caused by LTG and PHT.\n\n## Conclusion\n\n*HLA-A*3101* does not appear to be a genetic marker for LTG- and PHT-induced MPE reactions in Europeans. The predictive application of *HLA-A*3101* as a clinically relevant genetic marker for cADRs in Europeans appears to be specific to CBZ.\n\n## Supplementary Material\n\n**Background**\n\n**Results**\n\n**Conclusion**\n\n**Background**\n\n**Results**\n\n**Conclusion**\n\n## Acknowledgments\n\n**Financial & competing interests disclosure**\n\nThis work was supported by grants from the Medical Research Council (G0400126), the Wellcome Trust (084730), UCLH CRDC (F136), the National Institute for Health Research (08–08-SCC), Brainwave – the Irish Epilepsy Association/the Medical Research Charities Group of Ireland/Health Research Board award (2009/001), the National Society for Epilepsy, the Fonds National de la Recherche Scientifique and the Fonds Erasme pour la Recherche Médicale, Université Libre de Bruxelles (Belgium) and supported, in part, by Award Number RC2NS070344 from the National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke. {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01S1Z1amQ6293SCicYNaMfUU","type":"tool_result","content":"     1→# Genome-wide Association Identifies the T Gene as a Novel Asthma Pharmacogenetic Locus\n     2→\n     3→## Metadata\n     4→**Authors:** Kelan G Tantisira, Amy Damask, Stanley J Szefler, Brooke Schuemann, Amy Markezich, Jessica Su, Barbara Klanderman, Jody Sylvia, Rongling Wu, Fernando Martinez, Homer A Boushey, Vernon M Chinchilli, Dave Mauger, Scott T Weiss, Elliot Israel\n     5→**Journal:** American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine\n     6→**Date:** 2012 Jun 15\n     7→**DOI:** [10.1164/rccm.201111-2061OC](https://doi.org/10.1164/rccm.201111-2061OC)\n     8→**PMID:** 22538805\n     9→**PMCID:** PMC3381232\n    10→**URL:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3381232/\n    11→**PDF:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3381232/pdf/AJRCCM185121286.pdf](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3381232/pdf/AJRCCM185121286.pdf)\n    12→\n    13→## Abstract\n    14→\n    15→Rationale: To date, most studies aimed at discovering genetic factors influencing treatment response in asthma have focused on biologic candidate genes. Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) can rapidly identify novel pharmacogenetic loci.\n    16→\n    17→Objectives: To investigate if GWAS can identify novel pharmacogenetic loci in asthma.\n    18→\n    19→Methods: Using phenotypic and GWAS genotype data available through the NHLBI-funded Single-nucleotide polymorphism Health association-Asthma Resource Project, we analyzed differences in FEV1 in response to inhaled corticosteroids in 418 white subjects with asthma. Of the 444,088 single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) analyzed, the lowest 50 SNPs by P value were genotyped in an independent clinical trial population of 407 subjects with asthma.\n    20→\n    21→Measurements and Main Results: The lowest P value for the GWAS analysis was 2.09 × 10−6. Of the 47 SNPs successfully genotyped in the replication population, three were associated under the same genetic model in the same direction, including two of the top four SNPs ranked by P value. Combined P values for these SNPs were 1.06 × 10−5 for rs3127412 and 6.13 × 10−6 for rs6456042. Although these two were not located within a gene, they were tightly correlated with three variants mapping to potentially functional regions within the T gene. After genotyping, each T gene variant was also associated with lung function response to inhaled corticosteroids in each of the trials associated with rs3127412 and rs6456042 in the initial GWAS analysis. On average, there was a twofold to threefold difference in FEV1 response for those subjects homozygous for the wild-type versus mutant alleles for each T gene SNP.\n    22→\n    23→Conclusions: Genome-wide association has identified the T gene as a novel pharmacogenetic locus for inhaled corticosteroid response in asthma.\n    24→\n    25→Keywords: polymorphism, genome, pharmacogenomics, glucocorticoid\n    26→\n    27→## AT A GLANCE COMMENTARY\n    28→\n    29→## AT A GLANCE COMMENTARY\n    30→\n    31→### Scientific Knowledge on the Subject\n    32→\n    33→There is clear cut interindividual variability in the response to inhaled corticosteroid medications in asthma with a significant number of nonresponders. Pharmacogenetics seeks to understand the genetic determinants of interindividual variability in drug response, but relatively few asthma pharmacogenetic loci have been identified to date.\n    34→\n    35→### What This Study Adds to the Field\n    36→\n    37→This study provides the results of a genome-wide association analysis focusing on identifying novel determinants of inhaled corticosteroid response in asthma. By using correlation within the genome, this analysis has led to the identification of a novel harmacogenetic locus, the *T* gene.\n    38→\n    39→## Methods\n    40→\n    41→Detailed methods, including SHARP and replication clinical trial descriptions, are described in the online supplement.\n    42→\n    43→### Study Populations\n    44→\n    45→SHARP included Childhood Asthma Management Program (CAMP) ([16](#bib16)), Childhood Asthma Research and Education (CARE) network ([6](#bib6), [17](#bib17)), and Asthma Clinical Research Network (ACRN) ([18](#bib18)–[21](#bib21)) participants. CAMP, two of five CARE trials, and four of six ACRN trials enrolled subjects with ICS monotherapy treatment response and were included. Further information on SHARP can be found in dbGAP ([http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/sites/entrez?Db=gap](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/sites/entrez?Db=gap)).\n    46→\n    47→Replication was performed in an 8-week clinical trial conducted by Forest pharmaceuticals, which has been detailed in other pharmacogenetic analyses ([10](#bib10), [13](#bib13)).\n    48→\n    49→### Phenotype\n    50→\n    51→The primary outcome phenotype was prebronchodilator FEV_1_ while on ICS minus prebronchodilator FEV_1_ off ICS, typically the difference between a placebo run-in period to a point 6–12 weeks later after ICS therapy. Additionally, a “reversal group” of individuals on ICS therapy who were tapered off therapy during a trial protocol and a “mixed group” of individuals with FEV_1_ measured at the start and end of an ICS run-in were included. The mean (± SD) FEV_1_% difference was nearly identical for all three groups (4 ± 7.9, 4.3 ± 10.9, and 4.1 ± 11.8 percent for standard, reverse, and mixed groups, respectively) (*see* Figure E1 in the online supplement). Of 792 subjects with ICS response data, 458 were white, forming the phenotypic analysis group.\n    52→\n    53→### Genotyping\n    54→\n    55→GWAS genotyping was performed by Affymetrix (Santa Clara, CA) using a Human SNP Array 6.0. Marker quality control (QC) is detailed in the online supplement. Overall, 444,088 SNPs and 4,113 genotyped white subjects passed QC. After merger with the phenotypic dataset, the final ICS analysis group was 418: 179 ACRN, 147 CAMP, and 92 CARE.\n    56→\n    57→Replication genotyping was performed using Sequenom (San Diego, CA). *T* SNP genotyping was performed using Taqman (Applied Biosystems, Foster City, CA). Because additional DNA was not available in CARE and ACRN, corresponding *T* genotypes were inferred using imputation with Markov Chain Haplotyping ([22](#bib22)) based on HapMap Phase 2 Release 22 data ([23](#bib23)). The ratio of empirically observed to expected (binomial) dosage variance for these SNPs was greater than 0.9, indicating good quality of imputation.\n    58→\n    59→### Statistical Analysis\n    60→\n    61→GWAS analyses were performed using PLINK ([24](#bib24)). Given similarities between CAMP and CARE phenotypic data, these two datasets were pooled. ACRN data were analyzed separately. The separate β-estimates and standard errors were then used to create combined β-estimates and *P* values, using a random-effects approach ([25](#bib25)).\n    62→\n    63→The ICS analysis comprised two phenotypic definitions: difference in raw FEV_1_ scores (prefev_on___ICS_–prefev_off_ICS_) and difference in percent predicted FEV_1_ scores (prefev%_on_ICS_–prefev%_off_ICS_). The primary outcome, raw differences, was adjusted for age, height, sex, study group, and six significant principal components. The percent predicted differences were adjusted for study group and six significant principal components. ACRN analyses were further adjusted for ICS therapy timing (i.e., standard, reverse, and mixed).\n    64→\n    65→Generalized linear models evaluated the association between the selected SNPs and FEV_1_ change in the replication population_,_ adjusting for age, sex, and height using SAS (SAS Institute, Cary, NC). Analyses assumed an additive effect of the minor allele. A replication was defined by a nominal *P* less than 0.05.\n    66→\n    67→## Results\n    68→\n    69→### Populations\n    70→\n    71→A total of 418 SHARP subjects met phenotypic and genotypic inclusion criteria ([Table 1](#tbl1)). Of these, 239 pediatric CAMP and CARE subjects were pooled before analysis, whereas 179 adult ACRN subjects were analyzed separately. In addition to age, the adult SHARP subjects had a greater proportion of females and lower baseline FEV_1_ as a percent of predicted before ICS therapy compared with the pediatric subjects. To limit potential for associations related to occult population stratification, we limited analysis to subjects self-designated as whites. Characteristics of our replication population, Forest, are also shown in [Table 1](#tbl1). Overall, this population was similar to the SHARP ACRN subjects at baseline, with a more robust response to ICS therapy. This may be related, in part, to the low baseline FEV_1_ associated with this population.\n    72→\n    73→### TABLE 1.\n    74→\n    75→|   | ACRN* | CAMP* | CARE* | Forest† |\n    76→| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n    77→| N | 179‡ | 147 | 92 | 407 |\n    78→| Age ± SD | 33.8 ± 11.2 | 8.7 ± 2.1 | 11 ± 3.3 | 39.4 ± 13.6 |\n    79→| Sex (male) | 66 (37%) | 82 (56%) | 55 (60%) | 170 (42%) |\n    80→| Race (white)§ | 100% | 100% | 100% | 100% |\n    81→| Baseline FEV1% | 79 ± 15.8 | 94.2 ± 14.5 | 99.4 ± 12.6 | 71.7 ± 12.9 |\n    82→| Duration of ICS therapy | 6 wk | 8 wk | 6–8 wk | 8 wk |\n    83→| Mean FEV1% change after ICS | 4.1 ± 10.4 | 5.2 ± 11.5 | 4.6 ± 8.7 | 7.2 ± 19.7 |\n    84→\n    85→Table 1 Caption: NETWORK POPULATION DESCRIPTORS\n    86→\n    87→### Initial GWAS Association\n    88→\n    89→GWAS of the FEV_1_ difference related to ICS therapy was conducted on 444,088 SNPs in CAMP/CARE pooled samples and separately in the ACRN subjects. [Figure 1](#fig1) demonstrates a Manhattan plot of the combined association *P* values. For internal consistency, we then checked for association of the SNPs with differences in FEV_1_ as a percent predicted related to ICS therapy. The top 50 *P* values from the raw FEV_1_ difference analysis that were also associated with differences in predicted FEV_1_ in the combined analysis are shown in [Table 2](#tbl2) and were taken forward for genotyping in the replication population. Four SNPs had *P* values less than 1 × 10^−5^, although this fell short of the threshold for genome-wide significance.\n    90→\n    91→### Figure 1.\n    92→\n    93→![Figure 1.](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/3281/3381232/9b9df13dd861/AJRCCM185121286fig1.jpg)\n    94→\n    95→[View larger image](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=3381232_AJRCCM185121286fig1.jpg)\n    96→\n    97→Manhattan plot of genome-wide association study results. Plotted are the 444,088 P values (as negative log P values for association with difference in FEV1 after inhaled corticosteroid therapy) versus their chromosomal position on the genome.\n    98→\n 151→Table 2 Caption: TOP 50 SHARP P VALUES FOR FEV1 CHANGE\n   152→\n   153→### Replication Population Association\n   154→\n   155→Of the 50 SNPs, 47 were successfully genotyped in the replication population. Three SNPs were associated with FEV_1_ difference in response to flunisolide, under the assumption of an additive model ([Table 3](#tbl3)) in the same direction as the original SHARP analysis. [Table 3](#tbl3) also demonstrates the *P* value for overall association, reflecting the combined association *P* values for the ACRN, CAMP/CARE, and Forest populations, which was calculated using Stouffer z-transform test. Of note, two of the top four SHARP SNPs as ranked by *P* value (rs6456042 and rs3127412) were among the three SNPs replicating in the Forest population. After replication, the lowest combined *P* value was 6.13 × 10^−6^, which is below the threshold for genome-wide significance (1.13 × 10^−7^).\n   156→\n   157→### TABLE 3.\n   158→\n   159→|   | rs3127412 |  | rs6456042 |  | rs9955411 |  |\n   160→| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n   161→|   | Beta | P Value | Beta | P Value | Beta | P Value |\n   162→| SHARP (overall) | 0.078 | 9.68 × 10−6 | 0.079 | 6.67 × 10−6 | 0.080 | 0.00013 |\n   163→| ACRN | 0.090 | 0.034 | 0.103 | 0.013 | 0.096 | 0.05 |\n   164→| CAMP/CARE | 0.076 | 0.00013 | 0.074 | 0.0002 | 0.076 | 0.0011 |\n   165→| Forest | 0.071 | 0.02 | 0.072 | 0.02 | 0.092 | 0.02 |\n   166→| Combined P* |   | 1.06 × 10−5 |   | 6.13 × 10−6 |   | 4.64 × 10−5 |\n   167→\n   168→Table 3 Caption: ASSOCIATED REPLICATION SNPS\n   169→\n   170→### Mapping of Top SNPs\n   171→\n   172→Rs6456042 and rs3127412 are located on chromosome 6q27; both map to within 50 kb downstream of a transcription factor known as the *T* gene (Entrez Gene: 6,862). Using the CEU HapMap population, linkage disquilibrium (LD) (i.e., correlation between genotypes) measures were obtained for SNPs mapping to the *T* gene with rs6456042 and rs3127412. For these two SNPs, which are in tight LD, three *T* gene SNPs were correlated, with an *r*^2^ (correlation coefficient) greater than or equal to 0.66 (*see* Figure E2). Furthermore, each of these three *T* gene SNPs are located within potential functional regions of the gene: rs3099266 maps to the promoter region, rs1134481 to the 3′ untranslated region, and rs2305089 is a nonsynonymous variant coding for a glycine to aspartic acid amino acid change at position 177 within the gene. The SHARP genotype dataset did not contain any of these variants. Therefore, to elucidate whether the initial GWAS association actually represented a downstream correlation with a true association within the *T* gene, we genotyped these three SNPs in the CAMP and Forest populations, and analyzed imputed genotypes for the SNPs in ACRN and CARE.\n   173→\n   174→### T Gene Analysis\n   175→\n   176→Association analysis within CAMP and Forest demonstrated that each of the three *T* SNPs were also associated with FEV_1_ response to ICS therapy, with *P* values for CAMP and Forest, respectively, as follows: rs1134481 (0.0009 and 0.01); rs2305089 (0.004 and 0.02); and rs3099266 (0.004 and 0.01) ([Table 4](#tbl4)). The strongest association noted was with rs1134481. For this variant, homozygous wild-type subjects had an improvement in FEV_1_ as a percent of predicted of 2.8 ± 1.3 and 2.7 ± 1 versus 9.9 ± 2.1 and 5.3 ± 1.5 for those homozygous mutant for CAMP and Forest, respectively. Overall, after adjustment for age, sex, and height, each *T* SNP demonstrated a twofold to threefold difference in FEV_1_ response for those subjects homozygous for the wild-type versus mutant alleles in both populations (*see* Figure 3). Imputed analyses of the *T* variants were also consistent with association through LD, with a significant association noted for ACRN. Although the imputed *T* gene variants were not associated with ICS response in CARE, this is consistent with the primary SHARP GWAS results for the two SNPs in LD with the *T* gene variants (*see* Table E1). Although CARE demonstrated the highest baseline level of FEV_1_ ([Table 1](#tbl1)), adjustment for baseline FEV_1_ did not significantly alter the associations with the *T* gene SNPs (*see* Table E2).\n   177→\n   178→### TABLE 4.\n   179→\n   180→| SNP | Role | CAMP* | Forest* | ACRN† | CARE† | Combined‡ |\n   181→| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n   182→| rs1134481 | 3′ UTR | 0.0017 | 0.041 | 0.0037 | 0.58 | 1.57 × 10−5 |\n   183→| rs2305089 | Coding exon (Gly177Asp) | 0.0077 | 0.061 | 0.013 | 0.73 | 2.3 × 10−4 |\n   184→| rs3099266 | Promoter | 0.0087 | 0.036 | 0.0028 | 0.98 | 1.1 × 10−4 |\n   185→\n   186→Table 4 Caption: ASSOCIATION OF T GENE VARIANTS WITH ICS RESPONSE\n   187→\n   188→## Discussion\n   189→\n   190→GWAS holds tremendous promise because of the ability to discover new biology through simultaneous interrogation of variants across the entire genome. This is especially applicable to the field of drug response pharmacogenetics, where candidate gene studies have failed to explain enough of the genetic variation to allow predictive modeling. Through the SHARP project, we interrogated 444,088 SNP variants for their association with lung function response to ICS in a compilation of seven asthma clinical trials. We were able to replicate 3 of the top 50 GWAS SNPs in an independent population, including two of the top four SNPs.\n   191→\n   192→Although none of the replicated SNPs were located within a gene, the top two SNPs were in LD (correlated) with three known variants in the *T* gene. Using LD to identify disease genes takes advantage of the historical preservation of regions within the genome as they are passed down through generations ([26](#bib26), [27](#bib27)). Each of the three correlated *T* gene variants maps to a potentially functional region of the gene, with rs2305089 encoding for an amino acid change (Gly177Asp) within the T protein. Each was also significantly associated with ICS response, suggesting that the true biologic effect is likely mediated by this gene. There was a twofold to threefold difference in FEV_1_ response for those subjects homozygous for the wild-type versus mutant alleles for each *T* gene SNP in each of the genotyped populations (*see* Figure E3).\n   193→\n   194→Despite the compilation of studies within SHARP, our study design remains underpowered for finding associations of genome-wide significance. However, our approach used the GWAS as an initial step to subsequently target variation within the *T* gene locus, resulting in an effect that was replicated in a larger replication cohort and the combined population. Each of the three interrogated *T* SNPs was associated with ICS response even after correction for multiple comparisons.\n   195→\n   196→The *T* gene, which has not previously been implicated in either asthma or corticosteroid pathobiology, is a founding member of an ancient family of genes containing a common protein motif, the T locus ([28](#bib28)). The T locus encodes a product with DNA binding activity that likely plays a role in the development of all vertebrate organisms. The *T* gene itself (*Brachyury homolog*) encodes for a key mesodermal developmental transcription factor crucial to notochord development ([29](#bib29)). However, the expression of *T* is sufficiently early in the developmental process that it has the potential to influence mesodermal development overall ([30](#bib30)), including within the lung. Corticosteroids are also crucial to lung development; alterations in corticosteroid-responsive genes during development may thus influence later asthma susceptibility and treatment response. *T* is also expressed in normal adult lung tissue ([31](#bib31)), although little is known about its transcriptional regulation of other genes. *T* and other T-box genes have been implicated in the function of bone morphogenetic protein (BMP) and fibroblast growth factor (FGF) pathways ([32](#bib32)). Specifically, decreased *T* expression inhibits chondrogenesis mediated by *BMP2* and *FGFR3* ([33](#bib33)). Notably, the *BMP2* receptor and *FGFR3* have been independently implicated in corticosteroid resistance ([34](#bib34), [35](#bib35)), supporting a mechanistic basis for the *T* gene in ICS response in asthma.\n   197→\n   198→To further explore the potential effects of the *T* gene on response to corticosteroids, we conducted a pathway analysis specifically focusing on potential *T* gene–*NR3C1* (glucocorticoid receptor) interactions using the program GeneMania ([http://genemania.org/](http://genemania.org/)) (*see* Figure E4). Although not conclusive, the results indicate that the *T* gene is coexpressed with three other genes (*NRIP1*, *FOXA2*, and *TTPA*) that directly interact or are predicted to interact with *NR3C1*.\n   199→\n   200→Although variations in *T* have the potential to directly cause alterations in response to ICS therapy, the familial lineage of *T* also offers intriguing mechanistic possibilities. *T* is phylogenetically conserved with 50 known orthologs. Moreover, 16 other members of the T family are paralogous (i.e., the result of a prior gene duplication within the same ancestral chromosome), including genes encoding for a number of the T-box domain proteins, such as *TBX21* (data from Ensembl [www.ensembl.org]). It is possible that there is sufficient overlap in the genomic structure of *T* and one of these paralogs that the mechanism of action is actually by the paralog; we have previously reported an independent ICS pharmacogenetic association with airways responsiveness in asthma resulting from variation in *TBX21* ([11](#bib11)). *T* is known to interact with other members of the T-box family ([36](#bib36)), yielding an additional mechanism through which a *T* may affect ICS response. Additional work in defining the role of genetic variation within *T* and the role of *T* on asthma treatment response is warranted.\n   201→\n   202→Our top GWAS hits from this analysis contrast with those that we have recently identified by a family-based screening algorithm ([37](#bib37)). This is expected because the current analysis, given its larger sample size, ranked the SNPs by *P* value, whereas our prior study ranked SNPs by relative power for replication, which is only possible in a family-based study design. We also did not identify the *GLCCI1* SNP from our prior study ([37](#bib37)) as one of our most significant variants in this study; however, neither of the *GLCCI1* variants associated in the previous study is incorporated on the genotyping chip used in this study. Specifically, because our *GLCCI1* resequencing revealed only one other variant, rs37973, in tight LD with the original associated variant (rs37972) and because neither of these SNPs is present on the Affymetrix platform used for the current analysis, we did not *a priori* expect to replicate the *GLCCI1* association.\n   203→\n   204→There are several potential limitations to our study. As noted, we do not report any association exceeding the genome-wide significance threshold (Bonferroni adjusted). However, this adjustment may be overly conservative, because it does not account for LD within the genotyped populations. Additionally, our independent replication of the findings is reassuring of a true positive association. Moreover, the association of upstream variants within the *T* gene that were not in complete LD (*r*^2^ = 0.66–0.82) with the initial association lends further credence to the validity of the association. Although we used a compilation of different studies, the treatment response distribution based on timing of ICS usage, age (adult vs. child), and clinical network was similar (*see* Figure E1), suggesting that the data can be combined for genetic analyses. Moreover, findings noted across these distributions support the generalization of our results to multiple clinical scenarios.\n   205→\n   206→In conclusion, GWAS study of ICS response in SHARP has allowed the identification of a novel pharmacogenetic locus, the *T* gene. For variants within this gene, there was a twofold to threefold difference in FEV_1_ response for those subjects homozygous for the wild-type versus mutant alleles. As genome-wide studies identify additional variants influencing drug treatment response in asthma and other diseases, we will continue to draw closer to the era of truly personalized medicine.\n   207→\n   208→## Supplementary Material\n   209→\n   210→## Acknowledgments\n   211→\n   212→The full Single-Nucleotide Polymorphism Health Association-Asthma Resource Project acknowledgment can be found in the online supplement. The authors specifically acknowledge James Kiley for his leadership and funding contribution to this manuscript.\n   213→\n   214→## Footnotes\n   215→\n   216→## Associated Data\n   217→\n   218→*This section collects any data citations, data availability statements, or supplementary materials included in this article.*\n   219→\n   220→### Supplementary Materials\n   221→\n   222→### Supplementary Materials\n   223→\n   224→## References\n   225→\n   226→1. Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) can rapidly identify novel pharmacogenetic loci.\n\nObjectives: To investigate if GWAS can identify novel pharmacogenetic loci in asthma.\n\nMethods: Using phenotypic and GWAS genotype data available through the NHLBI-funded Single-nucleotide polymorphism Health association-Asthma Resource Project, we analyzed differences in FEV1 in response to inhaled corticosteroids in 418 white subjects with asthma. Of the 444,088 single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) analyzed, the lowest 50 SNPs by P value were genotyped in an independent clinical trial population of 407 subjects with asthma.\n\nMeasurements and Main Results: The lowest P value for the GWAS analysis was 2.09 × 10−6. Of the 47 SNPs successfully genotyped in the replication population, three were associated under the same genetic model in the same direction, including two of the top four SNPs ranked by P value. Combined P values for these SNPs were 1.06 × 10−5 for rs3127412 and 6.13 × 10−6 for rs6456042. Although these two were not located within a gene, they were tightly correlated with three variants mapping to potentially functional regions within the T gene. After genotyping, each T gene variant was also associated with lung function response to inhaled corticosteroids in each of the trials associated with rs3127412 and rs6456042 in the initial GWAS analysis. On average, there was a twofold to threefold difference in FEV1 response for those subjects homozygous for the wild-type versus mutant alleles for each T gene SNP.\n\nConclusions: Genome-wide association has identified the T gene as a novel pharmacogenetic locus for inhaled corticosteroid response in asthma.\n\nKeywords: polymorphism, genome, pharmacogenomics, glucocorticoid\n\n## AT A GLANCE COMMENTARY\n\n## AT A GLANCE COMMENTARY\n\n### Scientific Knowledge on the Subject\n\nThere is clear cut interindividual variability in the response to inhaled corticosteroid medications in asthma with a significant number of nonresponders. Pharmacogenetics seeks to understand the genetic determinants of interindividual variability in drug response, but relatively few asthma pharmacogenetic loci have been identified to date.\n\n### What This Study Adds to the Field\n\nThis study provides the results of a genome-wide association analysis focusing on identifying novel determinants of inhaled corticosteroid response in asthma. By using correlation within the genome, this analysis has led to the identification of a novel harmacogenetic locus, the *T* gene.\n\n## Methods\n\nDetailed methods, including SHARP and replication clinical trial descriptions, are described in the online supplement.\n\n### Study Populations\n\nSHARP included Childhood Asthma Management Program (CAMP) ([16](#bib16)), Childhood Asthma Research and Education (CARE) network ([6](#bib6), [17](#bib17)), and Asthma Clinical Research Network (ACRN) ([18](#bib18)–[21](#bib21)) participants. CAMP, two of five CARE trials, and four of six ACRN trials enrolled subjects with ICS monotherapy treatment response and were included. Further information on SHARP can be found in dbGAP ([http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/sites/entrez?Db=gap](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/sites/entrez?Db=gap)).\n\nReplication was performed in an 8-week clinical trial conducted by Forest pharmaceuticals, which has been detailed in other pharmacogenetic analyses ([10](#bib10), [13](#bib13)).\n\n### Phenotype\n\nThe primary outcome phenotype was prebronchodilator FEV_1_ while on ICS minus prebronchodilator FEV_1_ off ICS, typically the difference between a placebo run-in period to a point 6–12 weeks later after ICS therapy. Additionally, a “reversal group” of individuals on ICS therapy who were tapered off therapy during a trial protocol and a “mixed group” of individuals with FEV_1_ measured at the start and end of an ICS run-in were included. The mean (± SD) FEV_1_% difference was nearly identical for all three groups (4 ± 7.9, 4.3 ± 10.9, and 4.1 ± 11.8 percent for standard, reverse, and mixed groups, respectively) (*see* Figure E1 in the online supplement). Of 792 subjects with ICS response data, 458 were white, forming the phenotypic analysis group.\n\n### Genotyping\n\nGWAS genotyping was performed by Affymetrix (Santa Clara, CA) using a Human SNP Array 6.0. Marker quality control (QC) is detailed in the online supplement. Overall, 444,088 SNPs and 4,113 genotyped white subjects passed QC. After merger with the phenotypic dataset, the final ICS analysis group was 418: 179 ACRN, 147 CAMP, and 92 CARE.\n\nReplication genotyping was performed using Sequenom (San Diego, CA). *T* SNP genotyping was performed using Taqman (Applied Biosystems, Foster City, CA). Because additional DNA was not available in CARE and ACRN, corresponding *T* genotypes were inferred using imputation with Markov Chain Haplotyping ([22](#bib22)) based on HapMap Phase 2 Release 22 data ([23](#bib23)). The ratio of empirically observed to expected (binomial) dosage variance for these SNPs was greater than 0.9, indicating good quality of imputation.\n\n### Statistical Analysis\n\nGWAS analyses were performed using PLINK ([24](#bib24)). Given similarities between CAMP and CARE phenotypic data, these two datasets were pooled. ACRN data were analyzed separately. The separate β-estimates and standard errors were then used to create combined β-estimates and *P* values, using a random-effects approach ([25](#bib25)).\n\nThe ICS analysis comprised two phenotypic definitions: difference in raw FEV_1_ scores (prefev_on___ICS_–prefev_off_ICS_) and difference in percent predicted FEV_1_ scores (prefev%_on_ICS_–prefev%_off_ICS_). The primary outcome, raw differences, was adjusted for age, height, sex, study group, and six significant principal components. The percent predicted differences were adjusted for study group and six significant principal components. ACRN analyses were further adjusted for ICS therapy timing (i.e., standard, reverse, and mixed).\n\nGeneralized linear models evaluated the association between the selected SNPs and FEV_1_ change in the replication population_,_ adjusting for age, sex, and height using SAS (SAS Institute, Cary, NC). Analyses assumed an additive effect of the minor allele. A replication was defined by a nominal *P* less than 0.05.\n\n## Results\n\n### Populations\n\nA total of 418 SHARP subjects met phenotypic and genotypic inclusion criteria ([Table 1](#tbl1)). Of these, 239 pediatric CAMP and CARE subjects were pooled before analysis, whereas 179 adult ACRN subjects were analyzed separately. In addition to age, the adult SHARP subjects had a greater proportion of females and lower baseline FEV_1_ as a percent of predicted before ICS therapy compared with the pediatric subjects. To limit potential for associations related to occult population stratification, we limited analysis to subjects self-designated as whites. Characteristics of our replication population, Forest, are also shown in [Table 1](#tbl1). Overall, this population was similar to the SHARP ACRN subjects at baseline, with a more robust response to ICS therapy. This may be related, in part, to the low baseline FEV_1_ associated with this population.\n\n### TABLE 1.\n\n|   | ACRN* | CAMP* | CARE* | Forest† |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| N | 179‡ | 147 | 92 | 407 |\n| Age ± SD | 33.8 ± 11.2 | 8.7 ± 2.1 | 11 ± 3.3 | 39.4 ± 13.6 |\n| Sex (male) | 66 (37%) | 82 (56%) | 55 (60%) | 170 (42%) |\n| Race (white)§ | 100% | 100% | 100% | 100% |\n| Baseline FEV1% | 79 ± 15.8 | 94.2 ± 14.5 | 99.4 ± 12.6 | 71.7 ± 12.9 |\n| Duration of ICS therapy | 6 wk | 8 wk | 6–8 wk | 8 wk |\n| Mean FEV1% change after ICS | 4.1 ± 10.4 | 5.2 ± 11.5 | 4.6 ± 8.7 | 7.2 ± 19.7 |\n\nTable 1 Caption: NETWORK POPULATION DESCRIPTORS\n\n### Initial GWAS Association\n\nGWAS of the FEV_1_ difference related to ICS therapy was conducted on 444,088 SNPs in CAMP/CARE pooled samples and separately in the ACRN subjects. [Figure 1](#fig1) demonstrates a Manhattan plot of the combined association *P* values. For internal consistency, we then checked for association of the SNPs with differences in FEV_1_ as a percent predicted related to ICS therapy. The top 50 *P* values from the raw FEV_1_ difference analysis that were also associated with differences in predicted FEV_1_ in the combined analysis are shown in [Table 2](#tbl2) and were taken forward for genotyping in the replication population. Four SNPs had *P* values less than 1 × 10^−5^, although this fell short of the threshold for genome-wide significance.\n\n### Figure 1.\n\n![Figure 1.](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/3281/3381232/9b9df13dd861/AJRCCM185121286fig1.jpg)\n\n[View larger image](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=3381232_AJRCCM185121286fig1.jpg)\n\nManhattan plot of genome-wide association study results. Plotted are the 444,088 P values (as negative log P values for association with difference in FEV1 after inhaled corticosteroid therapy) versus their chromosomal position on the genome.\n\n### TABLE PLEKHA6 | 0.023 | 1.56 × 10−3 | 9.27 × 10−5 |\n| rs420828 | AC091947.4 | 0.242 | 1.86 × 10−4 | 9.28 × 10−5 |\n| rs1121121 | AC023481.6 | 0.290 | 1.87 × 10−4 | 9.68 × 10−5 |\n| rs11014839 |
